extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds on all reachable executions.

The only transition that can set sent:=true while opted_out is true is t4, guarded by (opted_out and acc == 14). t2 sets sent:=true only when not opted_out (cannot violate). t3 sets sent:=false. t1 (optout) sets sent:=false. The step transitions set sent:=false.

So a violation requires reaching a state with opted_out=true and acc==14, then firing send_attempt. But acc==14 is unreachable. acc starts at 0. Each step transition computes acc:=(3*acc + c) mod 32 (the triple nested-if subtracts 32 up to three times, which I verified exactly equals mod 32 for all acc in [0,31]), with c=31 (step_0), c=7 (step_1), c=23 (step_2). All three constants are congruent to 3 (mod 4). Hence every step does acc := (3*acc + 3) mod 4 = (-acc - 1) mod 4, which keeps acc in {0, 3} (mod 4) starting from 0. optout and send_attempt do not modify acc. Since 14 == 2 (mod 4), acc can never equal 14, so t4's guard is never satisfiable.

I confirmed this by exhaustive forward reachability over the full finite state space (opted_out x sent x acc): exactly 48 states are reachable, the reachable acc values are {0,3,4,7,8,11,12,15,16,19,20,23,24,27,28,31} (never 14), and zero reachable states have opted_out=true with sent=true. Because the system is deterministic per event and the reachable set is closed, this conclusion is independent of trace length and easily covers the 34-event bound. Therefore the property is never violated.
